one_line_takeaway: A three-stage LLM pipeline (rule-constrained extraction, anchor-word-based diversity scoring, entropy-constrained fusion) synthesizes inconsistent CVE/IBM/CNNVD/JVN vulnerability descriptions into a single 'Digest Label,' lifting fusion/extraction F1 into the 0.85-0.94 range and cutting LLM hallucination rates roughly in half versus vanilla and chain-of-thought LLM baselines, though the authors themselves flag the overall gain as moderate and the human evaluation as small in scale.
key_finding: The domain-constrained framework outperforms every baseline at each stage. Extraction F1 (rule-based reward, ERNIE): 0.91 Vulnerability Type, 0.92 Attack Vector, 0.94 Attacker Type, 0.91 Impact, 0.90 Root Cause — above regularization, NER, and in-context-learning baselines. Self-evaluation F1 ranges 0.82-0.85 for the anchor-word method vs. 0.79-0.84 for CSG/ITL glossary substitution and lower still for raw BERT similarity. Fusion F1 (Table 3, ERNIE): Ours reaches 0.90/0.83/0.92/0.85/0.85 for Vulnerability Type/Attack Vector/Attacker Type/Impact/Root Cause vs. 0.84/0.81/0.91/0.83/0.83 (no-heuristic) and 0.84/0.79/0.90/0.84/0.82 (BERT-similarity constraint). Overall average F1 across extraction+fusion+self-evaluation (Table 4): vanilla ERNIE+Alg.3 = 0.82, ERNIE+CoT (Alg.4) = 0.84, framework with GPT-3.5 = 0.86, framework with ERNIE = 0.87 (best). Gains are uneven across aspects and models: e.g. with ERNIE, Root Cause F1 rises 0.73->0.80, Impact 0.74->0.79, Attack Vector 0.71->0.84 (Table 5), while gains for Vulnerability Type/Attacker Type are smaller and the relative improvement shrinks on stronger base models (GPT-4 Root Cause only 0.76->0.81). The framework also reduces hallucination (Table 6) across all five tested LLMs, e.g. GPT-3.5's Root Cause hallucination rate drops from 0.22 to 0.14 and Attack Vector from 0.15 to 0.08; ERNIE has the highest baseline hallucination rates (up to 0.25 for Attack Vector) and shows the largest absolute reductions. Missing-rate consolidation: merging the four repositories lowers Root Cause missingness from a 0.49-0.66 per-repository range to 0.23, Vulnerability Type from 0.32 to 0.15, and Attack Vector from 0.20 to 0.09. In the human study, the Digest Labels condition produced a 31.3% increase in comprehension and a 33.5% increase in efficiency relative to the comparison groups (per the introduction/abstract; the paper's Section 5.5 describes the study design but this quoted PDF excerpt cuts off before the itemized per-group numeric results table).
core_contribution: The paper proposes a domain-constrained LLM-based synthesis framework for reconciling inconsistent 'key aspects' (Vulnerability Type, Attack Vector, Attacker Type, Root Cause, Impact) across multiple Textual Vulnerability Description (TVD) repositories (CVE, IBM X-Force, CNNVD, JVN). The framework has three stages: (1) Extraction via rule-based reward constraints (regularization templates replace in-context-learning examples), (2) Self-Evaluation using LLM-extracted domain-specific 'anchor words' plus BERT embeddings/cosine similarity to score cross-repository semantic diversity, and (3) Fusion via word-frequency-based information entropy injected as a soft constraint into the LLM prompt to merge sentences while discouraging over-merging/redundancy. On top of this framework the authors build 'Digest Labels' (DLs), a nutrition-label-style UI that consolidates the synthesized key aspects plus Integrity (missingness) and Diversity (inconsistency) radar-chart metrics for a given CVE-ID, and evaluate it with a 12-participant human study.
threat_model: Not applicable in the adversarial-attacker sense — this is a data-quality / information-synthesis paper about vulnerability metadata, not an attack or defense against an adversary. The 'threat' addressed is analyst-facing: incomplete or inconsistent CVE textual descriptions across repositories (CVE, IBM, CNNVD, JVN) causing security engineers to miss critical details (e.g., Root Cause) or waste time reconciling conflicting descriptions, which the authors argue can delay remediation and cause misinterpretation of risk. No adversarial attacker, exploit, or defense mechanism is modeled.
limitations: Authors' own caveats: (1) the overall framework gain is described as 'moderate,' and the authors explicitly state it 'remains unclear... why the improvement is not more substantial' and how much of the gain is attributable to underlying LLM capability versus the fusion algorithm itself. (2) The human evaluation is small-scale (12 participants, all from one academic CS/security-research institution — 9 graduate + 3 doctoral students) and the authors explicitly acknowledge this limits generalizability, noting industry practitioners were not included. (3) Non-English TVDs (JVN/Japanese, CNNVD/Chinese) were machine-translated via Google Translate before use, introducing a translation-fidelity dependency not further analyzed. (4) The hallucination evaluation uses a strict binary per-aspect criterion (any fabrication = hallucinated, no partial-credit distinction) on only 100 sampled CVE entries. (5) Ground-truth annotation required a calibration round because initial Fleiss' Kappa fell below 0.2 (poor agreement) on some samples, indicating the underlying labeling task is subjective/difficult even for domain experts. (6) The primary framework results are reported mainly for ERNIE-3.5, with other LLMs used only for the secondary fine-grained/hallucination analyses.

datasets_used: 289,105 CVE-IDs (1999-2023) yielding 956,619 TVDs scraped from CVE, IBM X-Force, CNNVD, and JVN

Stratified random evaluation sample of 384 CVE-IDs (95% confidence, 5% margin of error), distributed across 5 year-ranges (1999-2005 ... 2021-2023) and 6 software-type categories (OS, web apps/frameworks, DB systems, programming languages/libraries, middleware/virtualization, IoT/drivers)
100-CVE-entry subsample used specifically for the hallucination evaluation
quantitative_results: Motivation study inconsistency rate across key aspects: 0.36-0.67 (Table 1)
Root Cause missing rate: CVE 0.59, JVN 0.66; after 4-repository merge, missing rate drops to 0.23 (from a 0.49-0.66 per-repo range)
Vulnerability Type missing rate: 0.32 (single repo) -> 0.15 (merged); Attack Vector: 0.20 -> 0.09
Extraction F1 (rule-based reward, ERNIE): Vulnerability Type 0.91, Attack Vector 0.92, Attacker Type 0.94, Impact 0.91, Root Cause 0.90
Self-evaluation F1: proposed anchor-word method 0.82-0.85; CSG/ITL glossary baselines 0.79-0.84; BERT-similarity-only baseline lowest

Fusion F1 (Table 3, ERNIE): Ours 0.90/0.83/0.92/0.85/0.85 vs No-Heuristic 0.84/0.81/0.91/0.83/0.83 vs BERT-Similarity 0.84/0.79/0.90/0.84/0.82 (Vulnerability Type/Attack Vector/Attacker Type/Impact/Root Cause)
Overall average F1 (Table 4): ERNIE+vanilla(Alg.3)=0.82, ERNIE+CoT(Alg.4)=0.84, Ours(GPT-3.5)=0.86, Ours(ERNIE)=0.87
Per-aspect improvement with ERNIE (Table 5): Root Cause F1 0.73->0.80, Impact 0.74->0.79, Attack Vector 0.71->0.84; with GPT-4, Root Cause F1 0.76->0.81

Hallucination rate reduction (Table 6): GPT-3.5 Root Cause 0.22->0.14, Attack Vector 0.15->0.08; GPT-4 Root Cause 0.18->0.10; Deepseek Root Cause 0.23->0.15; ERNIE (highest baseline) Attack Vector 0.25->0.12; LLaMA-3 Root Cause 0.20->0.11
Inter-annotator agreement (Fleiss' Kappa): 0.806 extraction, 0.781 TVD evaluation, 0.835 fusion, 0.784 overall framework
Human study (abstract-level figures): 31.3% increase in comprehensiveness and 33.5% increase in efficiency for Digest Labels vs. comparison conditions

Average key-aspect text length: Vulnerability Type 5.1 words, Attacker Type 3.8 words, Attack Vector 13.6 words, Impact 9.7 words, Root Cause 8.4 words (~24 words/aspect, ~120 words total per CVE across all five aspects)
baselines_compared: Extraction: Regularization templates (Guo et al.), NER-based clustering models, example-based in-context learning
Self-evaluation: direct BERT cosine similarity, CSG (computer science glossary) substitution, ITL (IT terminology list) substitution
Fusion: No-Heuristic LLM merging (no entropy constraint), BERT-similarity-as-constraint merging
Overall pipeline: vanilla LLM workflow (Algorithm 3, no domain constraints), Chain-of-Thought prompting variant (Algorithm 4)
Cross-LLM generalization/hallucination comparisons: GPT-3.5, GPT-4, Deepseek-R1, ERNIE-3.5, LLaMA-3
